generated_oml:
// C3: Physical Sensing Components
instance CoreTemperatureSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Digital temperature sensor (e.g., DS18B20) measuring the core temperature of the insulated incubator container with high accuracy (±0.5°C) and wide measurement range (-55°C to +125°C). Placed to represent the thermal mass of the container. Samples at high frequency (1-10Hz) via I2C protocol to Raspberry Pi."
]

instance HeatbedProximitySensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Digital temperature sensor (e.g., DS18B20) monitoring localized temperature near the heatbed to capture thermal gradients and hotspots within the incubator. High precision (±0.1°C) with 1-Wire or I2C interface to Raspberry Pi."
]

instance AmbientTemperatureSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "External temperature sensor (e.g., DS18B20) placed outside the insulated container to monitor ambient conditions affecting thermal behavior. Lower sampling frequency (0.1-1Hz) due to slower ambient changes."
]

// C4: Physical-to-Virtual Interaction
instance CoreTemperatureDataTransmitted : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom CoreTemperatureSensor
    base:desc "Time-series analog temperature readings from the core sensor transmitted via I2C protocol to Raspberry Pi for DT calibration and real-time monitoring."
]

instance HeatbedProximityDataTransmitted : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom HeatbedProximitySensor
    base:desc "High-frequency temperature readings from the heatbed proximity sensor transmitted via I2C/1-Wire to Raspberry Pi for thermal gradient analysis."
]

instance AmbientTemperatureDataTransmitted : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom AmbientTemperatureSensor
    base:desc "Low-frequency ambient temperature readings transmitted via I2C to Raspberry Pi for environmental context in DT predictions."
]

// C10: Models/Data
instance SimplifiedThermalModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "First-order differential equation or lumped thermal model with two free parameters capturing core thermal dynamics. Simplified for computational efficiency but lacks granularity in heatbed thermal inertia representation."
]

instance HighFidelityThermalModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Second-order or distributed-parameter thermal model with four free parameters incorporating detailed heatbed thermal inertia. Validated through experimental comparison for superior accuracy."
]

instance SensorDataStream : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Aggregated time-series temperature data from all three sensors (two internal, one external) sampled at high frequency. Used for real-time model calibration and validation."
    DTDFVocab:fromData CoreTemperatureDataTransmitted, HeatbedProximityDataTransmitted, AmbientTemperatureDataTransmitted
]

instance ModelInputOutputData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Data flows from sensors to models for real-time simulation, with outputs used to inform monitors via cyber-physical feedback loop."
    DTDFVocab:fromData SensorDataStream
]

// C11: Enablers
instance CalibrationAlgorithm : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Optimization-based or machine learning-assisted calibration algorithm (e.g., least-squares, Bayesian inference) continuously updating model parameters (thermal inertia, heatbed efficiency) using real-time sensor data."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SimplifiedThermalModel, HighFidelityThermalModel
]

instance DifferentialEquationSolver : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Physics-based solver (e.g., SciPy in Python or MATLAB/Simulink) for resolving thermal dynamics equations in real-time. Enables predictive simulation of temperature trajectories."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo HighFidelityThermalModel
]

instance TimeSeriesForecastingModel : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Data-driven forecasting model (e.g., ARIMA, LSTM) for short-term temperature predictions, complementing physics-based models."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SensorDataStream
]

instance RaspberryPiController : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Embedded controller running Python scripts (RPi.GPIO, pigpio) for data acquisition, preprocessing (filtering, normalization), and transmission to DT framework. Handles sensor interfacing via GPIO/I2C."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SensorDataStream, ModelInputOutputData
]

instance PIDController : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Proportional-Integral-Derivative controller implemented on Raspberry Pi for real-time heatbed/fan actuation based on DT-generated setpoints."
]

instance StateEstimator : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Kalman filter or moving average-based estimator for denoising sensor data and generating high-fidelity state estimates for DT models."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SensorDataStream
]

instance SupervisoryController : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Rule-based system for high-level decision-making (e.g., switching between heating/cooling modes) based on DT insights and safety thresholds."
]

// C6: Services
instance RealTimeTemperatureMonitoring : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Continuous monitoring of internal/external temperatures using fused sensor data to generate a digital replica of the PT's thermal state."
    DTDFVocab:enables CalibrationAlgorithm, StateEstimator
]

instance ThermalAnomalyDetection : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Fault detection service identifying sensor failures, heatbed malfunctions, or insulation breaches via statistical thresholds or ML classifiers."
    DTDFVocab:enables CalibrationAlgorithm, SupervisoryController
]

instance PredictiveTemperatureSimulation : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Physics-based and data-driven simulation of temperature trajectories under current/perturbed conditions for what-if analysis."
    DTDFVocab:enables DifferentialEquationSolver, TimeSeriesForecastingModel
]

instance AdaptiveControlOptimization : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Dynamic tuning of PID gains or supervisory policies via optimization algorithms (gradient descent, RL) to minimize energy consumption while maintaining setpoints."
    DTDFVocab:enables PIDController, SupervisoryController
]

instance VisualizationDashboard : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Real-time 2D/3D visualizations (Grafana, Matplotlib) of temperature gradients, control signals, and historical trends for operators."
]

// C17: Insights/Actions
instance CoreTemperatureInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "High-fidelity estimate of the incubator's core temperature, calibrated against real-time sensor data and model predictions."
]

instance ThermalGradientInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Spatial temperature distribution within the incubator, highlighting hotspots near the heatbed and thermal gradients."
]

instance AmbientImpactInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Assessment of external environmental conditions (e.g., ambient temperature) affecting the incubator's thermal performance."
]

instance FaultDetectionAlert : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Alert triggered when anomalies (e.g., sensor drift, heatbed failure) are detected via statistical or ML-based methods."
]

instance ControlParameterAdjustment : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Automatic adjustment of PID controller gains or heatbed/fan activation cycles based on DT-generated optimization insights."
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance CorrectiveHeatingAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Manual or automatic intervention to adjust heatbed power or fan speed to correct temperature deviations from setpoints."
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic false
]

instance ModelRecalibrationTrigger : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Automatic initiation of calibration algorithms when model-prediction errors exceed predefined thresholds."
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

// C1: Acting Components
instance HeatbedActuator : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Electric resistive heating element embedded in the incubator's base, controlled via Raspberry Pi PWM signals for precise power modulation (0-100%)."
]

instance FanActuator : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Low-power DC fan for thermal distribution within the incubator, controlled via Raspberry Pi GPIO pins for on/off or variable-speed operation."
]

// C2: Environment
instance IncubatorEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:desc "Insulated styrofoam container housing the incubator's thermal regulation system."
    base:contains HeatbedActuator, FanActuator, CoreTemperatureSensor, HeatbedProximitySensor, AmbientTemperatureSensor
]

// C5: Components
instance RaspberryPiControllerComponent : DTDFVocab:Component [
    base:desc "Embedded controller running Raspberry Pi OS with Python scripts for data acquisition, preprocessing, and actuation control. Acts as the cyber-physical interface between sensors/actuators and DT framework."
]

instance DigitalTwinFramework : DTDFVocab:Component [
    base:desc "Custom-built DT pipeline integrating calibration algorithms, monitors, and actuation interfaces for closed-loop thermal regulation."
]

instance virtual_to_physical_interaction : DTDFVocab:VirtualToPhysical [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin (DT) exerts control over the Physical Twin (PT) via a **Raspberry Pi-based controller**, which executes commands to regulate the incubator's temperature. The control flow involves **setpoint adjustments** (target temperature values) and **actuator commands** (e.g., activating/deactivating the heatbed or fan). Command types include **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) control signals** or **model-predictive control (MPC) adjustments**, though the exact algorithm is not specified. Control message formats are not detailed, but the system likely uses **structured binary or JSON/ASCII messages** for clarity and compatibility with the Raspberry Pi's I/O interfaces. Validation and authentication mechanisms are not explicitly described, though the DT framework in **Figure 1 (Document 1)** implies **safety checks** (e.g., verifying commands against operational constraints) before execution. Feedback systems include **closed-loop monitoring** where the DT continuously compares simulated predictions with real-time sensor data to detect deviations (e.g., via fault detectors or state estimators). Conflict resolution is not addressed, but the DT’s **calibration algorithms** (Document 1) suggest dynamic adaptation to reconcile discrepancies between the PT’s behavior and the DT’s models. Command execution confirmation is implied through **sensor feedback loops** (e.g., verifying temperature stabilization post-command)."
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system for the incubator case study incorporates **real-time synchronization** between the Physical Twin (PT) and its Digital Twin (DT) via **sensor data collection** (temperature sensors inside and outside the insulated container) at unspecified but likely **high-frequency intervals** (e.g., sub-second or second-level granularity) to ensure accurate model calibration. The DT employs **calibration algorithms** to update its models (e.g., differential equation-based thermal models with 2–4 free parameters) in response to observed deviations between the PT and DT states. The temporal granularity of these updates depends on the **simulation speed** of the models, where more accurate models (e.g., the 4-parameter model) may require **higher computational latency** due to increased complexity. The system’s **monitoring and control loops** (e.g., supervisory controllers or fault detectors) operate at a time scale aligned with the PT’s dynamics (e.g., thermal inertia of the heatbed and styrofoam box), necessitating **low-latency data processing** to enable real-time adjustments. No explicit synchronization frequency or latency requirements are quantified in the documents, but the use of a **Raspberry Pi** for control suggests constraints on processing delays (e.g., <100ms for closed-loop stability)."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system for the incubator follows a **monolithic, model-centric architecture** where the core components are:
  1. **Data Layer**: Real-time sensor data (temperature) from the PT, collected via unspecified protocols (likely serial or network-based).
  2. **Model Layer**: Two **physics-based thermal models** (differential equation representations) with varying parameter counts (2 or 4), calibrated using **parameter estimation algorithms** to minimize PT-DT divergence.
  3. **Algorithm Layer**: **Calibration algorithms** (e.g., optimization techniques) to adjust model parameters, and **monitoring algorithms** (e.g., fault detectors or supervisory controllers) to evaluate PT performance against DT predictions. The **Raspberry Pi** acts as the **cyber-physical interface**, executing control actions derived from DT insights.
  The system lacks explicit **orchestration middleware** (e.g., message brokers or service orchestrators) but implies **tight coupling** between components. Resource management is implicit, with no mention of **scaling** or **fault tolerance** mechanisms. The DT’s **feedback loop** is closed via the controller, which adjusts the heatbed/fan based on DT-derived insights (e.g., predicted vs. actual temperature)."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in the provided context includes **pre-deployment phases** (modeling, simulation, and algorithm development) and **post-deployment phases** (monitoring, calibration, and real-time decision-making). During the **development stage**, abstract representations of the Physical Twin (PT) are created using **models** (e.g., CAD for geometric properties, differential equations for dynamic behavior) and **algorithms** (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers). These models are refined through **calibration algorithms** to align with real-world data collected via sensors. Post-deployment, the DT operates in a **closed-loop system** where real-time sensor data (e.g., temperature from the incubator case study) is fed into the DT to update models dynamically. The **transition management** between phases involves integrating models into the DT framework, ensuring seamless handover from design to operational phases. **Lifecycle governance** is implied through the use of **calibration** to maintain accuracy and **monitoring** to detect deviations, enabling corrective actions (e.g., adjustments to the PT via human or algorithmic intervention). The lifecycle emphasizes **continuous evolution** to adapt to changing environmental or operational conditions, as highlighted by the DT’s role in enabling reconfigurability and robustness."
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "
The incubator DT system emphasizes **model fidelity** and **validity** through the following technical approaches:

1. **Model Fidelity Requirements**:
   - **Trade-off Analysis**: Two models were developed to balance **accuracy** and **computational efficiency**:
     - **2-Parameter Model**: Lower fidelity but **faster simulation** (suitable for real-time control).
     - **4-Parameter Model**: Higher fidelity, capturing **thermal inertia** (critical for long-term predictions) but with **increased simulation time**.
   - **Selection Criteria**: The 4-parameter model was chosen for further DT development due to its **superior experimental validation** (e.g., closer alignment with PT’s thermal response during transient tests).

2. **Validation and Verification Methods**:
   - **Experimental Comparison**: Models were validated against **real-world data** from the PT, with **root-mean-square error (RMSE)** or **relative error metrics** used to quantify prediction accuracy.
   - **Calibration Techniques**: **Parameter optimization algorithms** (e.g., least squares, Bayesian inference) adjusted model parameters to minimize errors between simulated and measured temperatures.
   - **Closed-Loop Testing**: Models were integrated into the **control system** to verify their **practical utility** (e.g., maintaining setpoints under disturbances).

3. **Uncertainty Sources and Quantification**:
   - **Sensor Uncertainty**: Quantified via **manufacturer specs** (e.g., ±0.5°C for thermistors) and **empirical testing** (e.g., repeatability trials).
   - **Model Uncertainty**: Propagated through **Monte Carlo simulations** or **probabilistic modeling** to estimate prediction confidence intervals.
   - **Environmental Uncertainty**: Accounted for via **external sensor data** (ambient temperature) and **adaptive model parameters** (e.g., dynamic thermal conductivity adjustments).

4. **Error Detection and Correction Mechanisms**:
   - **Real-Time Monitoring**: **Anomaly detection algorithms** (e.g., statistical thresholds, machine learning classifiers) flag discrepancies between PT and DT (e.g., sudden temperature spikes/drops).
   - **Automatic Recalibration**: Triggers **parameter updates** when errors exceed predefined thresholds (e.g., RMSE > 1°C for 5 minutes).
   - **Fallback Modes**: If model validity degrades (e.g., due to sensor failure), the DT defaults to **simpler models** or **manual override** via the Raspberry Pi controller.

5. **Quality Assurance Processes**:
   - **Open-Source Validation**: The **public repository** ([GitHub](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)) enables **third-party verification** of hardware/software specifications and experimental results.
   - **Reproducibility**: Detailed documentation of **hardware setup**, **model equations**, and **experimental protocols** ensures consistency across implementations.
   - **Continuous Improvement**: Future work includes **expanding model complexity** (e.g., adding humidity or airflow dynamics) and **integrating advanced calibration techniques** (e.g., reinforcement learning).
"
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin (DT) technical connection for the incubator system relies on a **cyber-physical system (CPS) architecture** where the Raspberry Pi serves as the central node for communication between the cyber (digital) and physical (PT) components. The network topology is **star-shaped**, with the Raspberry Pi acting as the hub connecting sensors (temperature probes) and actuators (heatbed, fan). Communication protocols are not explicitly specified, but common choices for such systems include **MQTT (for lightweight IoT messaging), Modbus (for industrial control), or direct serial/UART links**. Bandwidth and latency requirements are not quantified, though the DT’s real-time monitoring and control demands **low-latency (<100ms) communication** to ensure timely adjustments. Reliability and redundancy mechanisms are not detailed, but the DT’s **calibration algorithms** (Document 1) imply fault tolerance by dynamically adjusting models to compensate for sensor/actuator failures. Security protocols are not mentioned, though standard practices (e.g., **TLS for encrypted communication, access control for the Raspberry Pi, or hardware-based authentication**) would likely be applied in a production deployment."
]

instance dt_hosting_deployment : DTDFVocab:Deployment [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin (DT) for the incubator system is deployed on a **Raspberry Pi (edge computing platform)**, which hosts both the **control algorithms and simulation models** locally. Computational requirements are modest, given the simplicity of the thermal models (e.g., differential equations with 2–4 free parameters), but the system must support **real-time calibration and simulation** to predict temperature dynamics accurately. Storage requirements are minimal, primarily for **sensor logs, model parameters, and calibration data**, though no specific capacities are provided. Scalability mechanisms are not addressed, as the case study focuses on a single incubator. Deployment models are **on-premise/edge-centric**, with no mention of cloud integration, though the DT’s modular structure (Document 1) suggests potential for hybrid deployment. Availability and reliability are ensured through **redundant sensor measurements** (two internal temperature sensors) and **model-based fault detection**, but no explicit redundancy for the Raspberry Pi or network infrastructure is described."
]
